exemplar.title: How Does the Resonant Frequency of a Wine Glass Vary with the Change in Volume of the Glass?

detailedExemplarData.title: How Does the Resonant Frequency of a Wine Glass Vary with the Change in Volume of the Glass?

subject.title: Physics
subject.description:
**IB Physics** is a **Group 4** subject in the **International Baccalaureate Diploma Programme (IBDP)**. It explores **space, time and motion; the particulate nature of matter; wave behaviour; fields; and nuclear and quantum physics**, with a strong focus on **experimental work, data analysis, and scientific investigation skills**.

The course is available at **Standard Level (SL, 150 teaching hours)** and **Higher Level (HL, 240 teaching hours)**, with **first assessment in 2025**.

### Assessment at a Glance

| Assessment | Format | SL | HL | Weighting |
|------------|--------|----|----|-----------|
| **Paper 1** | Multiple-choice questions and data-based questions | 1 hour 30 minutes | 1 hour 30 minutes | 30% |
| **Paper 2** | Short-answer and extended-response questions | 1 hour 30 minutes | 2 hours 15 minutes | 50% |
| **Internal Assessment** | Individual scientific investigation | 10 hours | 10 hours | 20% |

strengths: Data are presented in clearly labelled tables and graphs with error bars and averages
Processing (averaging, plotting bestâfit line) is internally consistent and numerically correct
Uncertainties in raw measurements are stated and visualised
weaknesses: No propagation of uncertainties into derived quantities or trend parameters
Regression analysis (trendline equation, RÂ², model fit) is missing
Limited detail on spectrumâanalysis settings (window size, peak detection)
feedback.calculationMethod: Holistic AI evaluation: Although data presentation is both clear and precise (band 5) and processing is competent (band 4), the major omissions in uncertainty propagation and lack of quantitative regression keep the work firmly in the 3â4 band. A score of 3 reflects solid basics with significant analytical gaps.

strengths: Clear and specific context linking the research question to applications in acoustics and sensor design
Wellâstructured, reproducible procedure with comprehensive equipment list and photographic documentation
Solid explanation of controlled variables, number of trials, and basic uncertainty considerations
weaknesses: Insufficient justification for the chosen waterâvolume range and number of trials
Ambiguities remain around striker force and microphone placement
Discussion of methodological considerations is descriptive rather than fully explanatory
feedback.calculationMethod: Holistic AI evaluation: The criterion combines a very strong, specific contextualisation (band 5) with a clearly reproducible procedure (upper band 3â4) and described but not explained methodological considerations (band 3). Averaging these performance levels and acknowledging the remaining ambiguities yields a holistic score of 4 out of 6.

strengths: Conclusion correctly identifies and restates the inverseâsquareâroot relationship
Links findings to theoretical model and practical applications in acoustics and sensing
Demonstrates consistency between data trends and underlying physics
weaknesses: Lacks quantitative comparison of fitted constants or percent deviations from theoretical values
Discussion of damping remains qualitative, without measured Q-factor or peakâwidth analysis
No numerical validation against literature or theoretical predictions
feedback.calculationMethod: Holistic AI evaluation: The conclusion is coherent and consistent with the data (band 4) but remains largely qualitative with no quantitative validation or rigorous scientific comparison (band 3). On balance, this warrants a holistic score of 3 out of 6.

strengths: Identifies and describes multiple specific methodological weaknesses (instrument precision, ambient noise, volume granularity)
Proposes realistic and relevant improvements (calibrated microphone, mechanical striker, finer volume steps)
Demonstrates clear linkage between each limitation and corresponding remedy
weaknesses: Does not prioritise weaknesses by relative impact or quantify their influence
Explanations of improvements are concise rather than deeply analytical or justified
Evaluation could be strengthened by ranking or weighting suggested changes
feedback.calculationMethod: Holistic AI evaluation: The evaluation describes specific limitations and offers realistic improvements, aligning with the 3â4 band. However, without prioritisation or detailed explanation of relative impact, the work sits midâband. A score of 3 reflects a balanced but not fully developed evaluation.

strandSelections.reasoning: The research question is clearly stated and embedded in a **specific context** â the resonance of a particular wine-glass geometry when water volume is varied.  The introduction links the investigation to relevant applications (liquid-level sensors, musical acoustics, structural design) and briefly outlines the governing physics equation, demonstrating that the student situates the question within an **appropriate and focused scientific setting**.  Minor stylistic issues exist, but they do not detract from the contextualisation, meriting placement in the 5â6 band; a score of 5 is awarded because the context, while specific, lacks a brief justification for the chosen volume range and glass type that would mark the very top of the band.

Uncertainties in volume (Â±5 mL) and frequency (Â±2 Hz) are quoted and represented with error bars; qualitative sources of error are listed.  However, there is **no propagation** of uncertainties into derived quantities, no discussion of percentage error, and no link between uncertainty size and the reliability of the trend.  Significant omissions keep the work in the 3â4 band; a score of 3 reflects these gaps.

Methodological considerations (controlled variables, rationale for three trials, basic uncertainty discussion) are **described** rather than fully **explained**.  The student recognises temperature, striking position and ambient noise but does not justify why three trials are statistically sufficient, why 25 mL steps capture the physics adequately, or how the small frequency shift (~15 Hz) ensures a measurable signal-to-noise ratio.  This corresponds to the mid-band (3).

Specific methodological weaknesses (microphone precision, ambient noise, striking inconsistency, volume granularity, temperature) are **described** and their qualitative impact on data quality is discussed.  The explanation does not prioritise or quantify the relative magnitude of each weakness, so the work sits solidly in the 3â4 band; 4 is appropriate given the breadth of issues identified.

The student provides a sequential procedure (baseline, 25 mL increments, three repeats, Audacity analysis) with enough detail that another student could **reasonably reproduce** the investigation.  Photographs of the set-up, a full equipment list and descriptions of how each reading is obtained are included.  However, some **ambiguities remain** (undefined striker force, unspecified microphone distance, no calibration method for smartphone audio), preventing full replication to the letter.  Hence, the work meets the 3â4 band and is judged at the upper end (4).

Data processing involves averaging three trials, plotting error bars and a line of best fit.  Calculations are internally consistent and numerically correct; nevertheless, the student stops short of a quantitative inverse-square-root fit or statistical analysis (RÂ², gradient uncertainty).  This is competent but not fully rigorous, fitting the upper part of the 3â4 band (4).

For every limitation listed, the student proposes a **realistic and relevant** improvement (e.g., calibrated microphone, soundproof room, mechanical striker, finer volume increments).  Some rationale is supplied, but the explanations remain concise rather than deeply analytical, aligning with the descriptor for 3â4 marks; a 4 is granted to reflect the clear link between weakness and remedy.

Data are organised in a **clearly labelled table** with units, average values and stated uncertainties, and two well-annotated graphs with error bars and best-fit lines.  Despite minor truncation in some column headings, the presentation remains both **clear and precise**, satisfying the 5â6 band; a 5 is assigned because figure captions and axis scaling could have been more professionally formatted.

The student links findings to the standard mass-frequency relationship and mentions damping theory, citing several sources.  However, the comparison is **descriptive**, lacking numerical validation against literature values or models.  Consequently, it meets but does not exceed the 3â4 band; a 3 is awarded.

The conclusion restates the observed inverse relationship and references the theoretical model, directly tying back to the data presented.  While coherent and consistent, it remains largely qualitative; no quantitative comparison (e.g., fitting constant, % deviation) is offered.  Thus the strand meets the 3â4 descriptor at the top end (4).

annotations.comment: The title succinctly states the variables and focus of the study, aiding reader comprehension and setting clear expectations for the investigation.
The introduction effectively situates the investigation within applications (sensors, acoustics, engineering), demonstrating specific context and relevance.
The background lists relevant applications but omits justification for the chosen volume range (0â150 mL). Add rationale to clarify why increments capture the physics adequately.
The research question is clearly articulated, specifying both the dependent variable (resonant frequency) and the independent variable (water volume), which supports reproducibility.
The hypothesis is well structured into two clear mechanisms, demonstrating understanding of mass and damping effects on resonant frequency.
Consider adding a quantitative prediction (e.g. expected frequency change per 25 mL) alongside $$f=\frac{1}{2\pi}\sqrt{\frac{k}{m}}$$ to make the hypothesis more testable.
The description of spectrum analysis in Audacity is brief; include details such as window size or peak detection method to improve clarity and precision.
The data table is well organised with clear averages and uncertainties, facilitating interpretation and comparison of results.
The mathematical expression for $$f=\frac{1}{2\pi}\sqrt{\frac{k}{m}}$$ should be formatted in LaTeX consistently, and each termâs physical meaning briefly restated.
The list of controlled variables omits quantification of striking force. Specify the force or method (e.g. spring-loaded striker) to enhance reproducibility.
Column headings like âFrequen cyâ are truncated, which reduces clarity. Adjust formatting so labels are fully visible.
Uncertainties are listed qualitatively but not propagated into derived quantities. Include propagation of Â±5 mL and Â±2 Hz through calculations to quantify overall error.
Axes and error bars are clearly defined, enhancing communication of data uncertainties.
The materials list is comprehensive. To improve clarity, include microphone specifications and recording distance to ensure another investigator can replicate setup.
Specify the exact distance between the phoneâs microphone and the glass rim to eliminate ambiguity in recording conditions.
Repeating each measurement three times supports averaging and basic uncertainty estimation, strengthening data reliability.
The discussion of damping is qualitative; include a measure of resonance peak width or decay rate (Q-factor) to support statements on energy absorption.
The graph lacks a trendline equation and coefficient of determination. Add a fit of the form $$f=Am^{-1/2}$$ and report RÂ² to quantify model agreement.
Observation strongly links the inverse square root trend to data via $$f\propto1/\sqrt{m}$$, demonstrating consistency between theory and results.
No regression analysis is shown to validate $$f=\frac{1}{2\pi}\sqrt{\frac{k}{m}}$$ against data. Perform a fit and report fit parameters to strengthen the mathematical analysis.
Proposed improvements use realistic measures (e.g., calibrated microphone, anechoic chamber), directly addressing identified limitations.
Environmental interferences are noted but not ranked by impact. Prioritise sources (e.g., noise vs. temperature) to focus improvements where most beneficial.
Practical implications effectively connect findings to applications in acoustics and sensor technology, demonstrating broader scientific context.
Link the slope of your data trend to the theoretical constant $$\tfrac{1}{2\pi}\sqrt{k}$$ by comparing fitted A value with expected stiffness parameter.
Evaluation lists specific limitations such as instrumentation and environment, demonstrating clear recognition of methodological weaknesses.
Using 25 mL steps shows clear trends but may overlook finer variations. Employ smaller increments (e.g., 5â10 mL) for improved resolution and curve fitting.
Temperature effects are acknowledged and a compensation method suggested. Measuring and correcting frequency for temperature variations would strengthen the analysis.
The final conclusion restates key findings but lacks numerical validation against theoretical predictions. Include percent deviation or fitted constants for full justification.
Key takeaways concisely summarise the inverse square root trend and damping effect, reinforcing consistency between results and theory.
Citation list is extensive but formatting varies. Adopt a consistent citation style (e.g., APA) to improve professionalism and readability.

rubricData.name: Physics IA
rubricData.rubricSpecificInfo: âThe Physics Internal Assessment (IA) is a critical component of the IB Diploma Programme, providing students with the opportunity to design and conduct independent investigations into physics phenomena. This assessment emphasizes the development of practical skills, analytical thinking, and a deep understanding of scientific methodologies. By engaging in the IA, students not only apply theoretical knowledge to real-world scenarios but also cultivate competencies in research design, data analysis, and critical evaluation, all of which are essential for academic and professional success in scientific fields. â

similarExemplars.title:
To what extent does decreasing the volume of air in a champagne bottle by adding water inside
affect the resonant frequency that is produced when the glass is set into an air-induced vibration
by blowing air across the opening of the neck?

by blowing air across the opening of the neck?

similarExemplars.title:
How does the length of the pipe affect the natural frequency of the air column in the pipe at constant air temperature when the pipe is:
a)	open at both ends and air is blown close to one end?
b)	open at one and closed at the other end, and air is blown close to the open end?
Natural frequency is the frequency that has the highest intensity and is the loudest of all frequencies which are produced with air blowing.

b)	open at one and closed at the other end, and air is blown close to the open end?
Natural frequency is the frequency that has the highest intensity and is the loudest of all frequencies which are produced with air blowing.
